required.title: [Listen Together] A diverse recorded music collection spanning multiple traditions

suggested.title: [Hands-On] A drum circle or group percussion class
[Watch Together] Videos of drum ensembles from various traditions

competence_markers.description: The child can maintain a steady beat by clapping or tapping for 30 seconds without external reference (metronome, music), staying within an acceptable tempo range
The child can echo rhythmic patterns of four to six beats accurately in call-and-response exercises
The child can clap or play at different tempos (fast, medium, slow) and maintain steadiness at each
The child can control dynamics on a percussion instrument — playing distinctly louder and softer on command
The child can move in time to music at varying tempos, demonstrating that the beat is felt in the body
The child can create and repeat a simple rhythmic pattern of their own invention
The child can play a simple part in a group percussion activity, maintaining their pattern while others play different patterns
The child demonstrates engagement and enthusiasm for rhythmic activities, not resistance or shutdown

narration_prompts.prompt: What is a beat? Show me a steady beat and tell me what makes it steady.
narration_prompts.detail: 2. **Clap a pattern for me, then change one thing about it. Tell me what you changed and how it sounds different.**

narration_prompts.prompt: What happens when music gets faster? What happens when it gets slower? Show me with clapping and tell me what it feels like.
narration_prompts.detail: 4. **Play your drum loud, then play it soft. What do you have to do differently with your hands? Why does it matter that you can control this?**

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about one kind of music we listened to from another part of the world. What did you notice about the rhythm? How was it different from other music you know?
narration_prompts.detail: 6. **When you play rhythm with other people, what do you have to do besides just play your own part?**

cross_connections.description: Rhythm is fundamentally physical. The coordination, timing, and body awareness developed in movement training directly support the physical demands of rhythmic work. The child who is learning to control their body in space is also learning to control their body in time.

Movement to music in this packet is the entry point for the more structured dance work in 2.1.7. The child who has internalized steady pulse and tempo flexibility through 3.4.1 arrives at dance with a rhythmic foundation already in place.

Keeping a steady beat requires self-control — the discipline to maintain consistency rather than speeding up or losing focus. The habits formed in IC1.1 are exercised directly in every rhythmic practice session.

Rhythm is the time dimension of music; 3.4.2 develops the pitch dimension. Together they form the complete foundation of musical understanding. The child's rhythmic training makes the listening work in 3.4.2 more precise and embodied.

The rhythmic foundation built here is essential for all performance work. The child who cannot keep a steady beat cannot sing in time, cannot play an instrument in an ensemble, and cannot perform music with others.
Language has rhythm — stress patterns, syllable lengths, the cadence of sentences. The child developing rhythmic awareness is also developing sensitivity to the prosody of language.

content.why_this_matters:
Everything in the world has a rhythm. Your heart beats in a steady pattern. You breathe in and out. When you walk, your feet alternate — left, right, left, right — and each step takes the same amount of time if you are walking steadily. Before you ever heard a song, before you ever clapped your hands to music, rhythm was already inside you.

Rhythm is the foundation of all music. Before anyone sang a melody or played a chord, there was a beat — a pulse that organized sound in time. Every song you have ever heard, every piece of music ever played, is built on rhythm. A song without melody is still music. A song without rhythm is nothing — it falls apart, because there is nothing holding it together.

In this packet, you are going to learn to feel rhythm in your body, keep a steady beat, listen for patterns, and move to music from all over the world. You will clap, stomp, tap, drum, march, and dance. You will learn that your body is your first instrument — the one you were born with — and that rhythm is something you practice and develop, not something you either have or do not have. Everyone can learn rhythm. It begins with paying attention to the beat.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **A reliable internal pulse** — the ability to feel and maintain a steady beat without external help, which is the single most important musical skill
2. **Tempo awareness** — understanding that speed changes the character and feeling of rhythm, not just how fast it goes
3. **Dynamic control** — the physical skill of playing or clapping louder and softer on purpose, not by accident
4. **Pattern recognition and repetition** — hearing a rhythmic pattern and reproducing it accurately, then varying it intentionally
5. **Movement to music** — the ability to move in time with music from different traditions and tempos
6. **Call-and-response skill** — echoing rhythmic patterns of increasing complexity, which trains both the ear and the hands
7. **Basic percussion competence** — producing sound from simple instruments with control of timing, volume, and character
content.core_content:
### Part 1: The Beat Inside You

Put your hand on your chest. Feel that? That is your heartbeat. It is steady. It does not speed up and slow down randomly — it keeps a pulse. Now breathe slowly. In... out... in... out. That is another rhythm. Your whole body runs on rhythms — your heart, your breathing, your walking, your sleeping and waking.

When we talk about rhythm in music, we start with the **beat** — the steady pulse underneath everything else. Think of it like the ticking of a clock: tick, tick, tick, tick. Every tick is the same distance from the last one. That steady ticking is the beat.

**The most important thing you will learn in this packet:** keeping a steady beat. This sounds easy. It is not. Try this: clap your hands at a steady pace — not too fast, not too slow — and keep going for thirty seconds without speeding up or slowing down. No music, no metronome, just you and your clapping. Most people speed up without realizing it. Keeping a truly steady beat is a skill, and it takes practice.

### Part 2: Fast and Slow — Tempo

The speed of the beat is called **tempo**. A fast tempo feels energetic, exciting, maybe even frantic. A slow tempo feels calm, heavy, solemn, or sleepy. The same rhythm at different tempos feels completely different.

Try this: clap a steady beat at a walking pace. Now clap the same beat faster — like you are running. Now slow it down — like you are moving through thick mud. Notice how the feeling changes even though the pattern is the same. Tempo is not just speed. It is mood.

When you listen to music, pay attention to the tempo. A lullaby is slow because it is trying to calm you. A march is at a walking pace because people need to walk to it. A dance might be fast because the dancers are excited and full of energy. The composer chose that tempo for a reason.

### Part 3: Loud and Soft — Dynamics

Hit a drum hard. Now hit it softly. The pattern can be the same, but the sound is completely different. The difference between loud and soft in music is called **dynamics**.

Here is the important thing about dynamics: controlling them is a skill. Anyone can hit a drum hard. But can you hit it at exactly the same medium volume ten times in a row? Can you start soft and get gradually louder, evenly, without any sudden jumps? Can you play as softly as possible without losing the beat? That is control. That is what musicians practice.

Your hands, your sticks, your shakers — they do what you tell them, but only if you have practiced enough that your body knows the difference between loud, medium, and soft. This is physical training, like learning to throw a ball at different distances.

### Part 4: Patterns — The Building Blocks of Rhythm

A beat is just a steady pulse. A **rhythm** is a pattern made from that pulse — some beats are longer, some shorter, some are silent. Think of it like building with blocks: the beat gives you the grid, and the rhythm gives you the shape.

Here is a simple pattern: **short-short-long**. Clap it: *clap-clap-claaap*. Now repeat it: *clap-clap-claaap, clap-clap-claaap*. You have just performed a rhythmic pattern.

Now try **long-short-short**: *claaap-clap-clap*. Feel how different it is? The notes are the same lengths, but the order changed, and it feels completely different.

Rhythmic patterns are everywhere. The rhythm of the words "Mississippi" is different from the rhythm of the word "Ohio." Tap them out and hear the difference. Language has rhythm. Walking has rhythm. Everything that moves in time has rhythm.

### Part 5: Call and Response — The Oldest Music Lesson

One person claps a pattern. Another person echoes it back exactly. This is **call and response**, and it is the oldest way human beings have taught music to each other. It works because it trains your ear and your body at the same time — you hear the pattern, hold it in your memory for a moment, and then reproduce it with your hands.

Start simple: the caller claps two beats. The responder echoes them. Then three beats. Then four. Then patterns with mixed long and short notes. Then patterns with a loud beat and quiet beats. Each time the pattern gets a little harder, your ear gets a little sharper and your hands get a little more skilled.

Call and response is also how much of the world's music works. In West African drumming, a lead drummer plays a call pattern and the ensemble responds. In gospel music, a preacher sings a line and the congregation answers. In military cadences, a leader calls and the group responds. This is not just a practice exercise — it is a musical tradition that spans the globe.

### Part 6: Moving to Music

Your body wants to move when it hears rhythm. That is not a distraction — that is the point. Rhythm lives in the body, and the best way to understand it is to move with it.

You will move to many different kinds of music in this packet. When you hear a march, you will march — left, right, left, right, in time with the beat. When you hear a waltz, you will sway — ONE-two-three, ONE-two-three. When you hear a drum ensemble, you will step and clap and feel the pattern in your whole body.

The goal is not to look a certain way. The goal is to be in time — to feel the beat so clearly that your body moves with it naturally, without thinking about it. A person who can move in time to music from any tradition has developed something valuable: an internal sense of time that is reliable, flexible, and connected to the body.

### Part 7: Percussion — Your First Instruments

A drum is simple: you hit it, it makes a sound. But there is a world of difference between banging on a drum and playing a drum. The difference is control.

When you play a hand drum, notice: hitting the center makes a deep sound (the **bass**). Hitting near the edge makes a higher, sharper sound (the **tone**). Tapping lightly with the fingertips makes a crisp, quiet sound (the **slap**). Three different sounds from the same drum, depending on where and how you strike it.

Rhythm sticks click. Shakers shake. Tambourines jingle and thump. Wood blocks make a hollow knock. Each instrument has its own voice — its own **timbre** — and choosing which instrument to play when is a musical decision.

When you play percussion in a group, you must listen as much as you play. Your job is not just to keep your own beat — it is to keep your beat together with everyone else's. This is harder than playing alone, and it is more important. Music is almost always made with other people. Learning to listen while you play is one of the most valuable skills rhythm training develops.
content.formative_practices:
### Activity 1: The Steady Beat Challenge [Ongoing — Every Session]
Begin every session by establishing a steady beat. The child claps, taps, or plays a drum at a consistent tempo for 30 seconds. Use a metronome to check. Over time, the child's internal clock becomes more reliable. This is the most important exercise in the packet and should never be skipped.

### Activity 2: Tempo Exploration [Early Packet]
Play the same clapping pattern at different tempos. March around the room at a walking pace, then speed up to a jog, then slow down to a crawl — all while keeping the beat steady at each tempo. Listen to music at different tempos and identify: is this fast, medium, or slow? What does the tempo make you feel?

### Activity 3: Call and Response Sessions [Ongoing]
The mentor claps a rhythmic pattern. The child echoes it. Start with two-beat patterns and build to six or more beats. Include variations in dynamics (loud and soft) and pattern complexity (short-short-long, long-short-short-short, etc.). Aim for at least five minutes of call-and-response in every session.

### Activity 4: Movement and Listening [Ongoing]
Put on a piece of music and move to it. Different music each session. March to a march. Sway to a waltz. Step to a drum pattern. Freeze when the music stops. Move slowly when the music is slow. The child is learning to hear tempo and feel it in the body simultaneously.

### Activity 5: Dynamics Drum Game [Mid-Packet]
Using a hand drum, practice the full range of volume: as loud as possible, as soft as possible, and three levels in between. Then play a "dynamics game": the mentor holds their hand at different heights — high means loud, low means soft — and the child plays the drum at the matching volume. This trains dynamic control as a physical, responsive skill.

### Activity 6: World Rhythms Listening [Ongoing]
Listen to rhythmic music from at least five different traditions over the course of the packet: West African drumming, Japanese taiko, Brazilian samba, a Sousa march, a rock-and-roll song, a folk dance from any tradition. For each, discuss: what do you notice about the beat? Is it fast or slow? Does it make you want to move? How?

### Activity 7: Pattern Building [Late Packet]
The child creates their own rhythmic patterns using clapping or a drum. Start by inventing a four-beat pattern. Repeat it four times. Then change one thing — make one beat longer, or add a rest (silence where a beat would be). This is the beginning of rhythmic composition: making something new on purpose.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Steady Pulse
**Developing well:** The child maintains a consistent beat for 30 seconds. When checked against a metronome, the tempo drifts by no more than a few beats per minute. The child is not counting or thinking hard — the pulse feels natural.

**Developing slowly:** The child speeds up consistently, or the beat is erratic with frequent stumbles. They cannot maintain tempo without an external reference.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the child practicing pulse at every session? Are you using the metronome regularly so the child can hear the difference between their internal tempo and a stable external reference? Is the practice happening when the child is alert and focused?

### 2. Call-and-Response Accuracy
**Developing well:** The child echoes four-to-six-beat patterns accurately, including patterns with mixed note values (short-short-long, long-short-short-short). They reproduce dynamics as well as rhythm.

**Developing slowly:** The child can echo two-beat patterns but struggles with longer or more complex ones. They consistently miss the last beat or add extra beats.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are the patterns appropriately challenging? The child should succeed about 70-80% of the time — enough success to build confidence, enough failure to build skill. Are you starting each session with easy patterns and building up?

### 3. Movement in Time
**Developing well:** The child moves naturally with music at various tempos. They can march in time to a march, sway to a waltz, and adjust when the tempo changes. Movement is relaxed and confident, not stiff or forced.

**Developing slowly:** The child moves randomly rather than in time with the music. They cannot consistently step on the beat even with familiar music at a comfortable tempo.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the child getting enough movement time? Some children need many sessions of free movement before structured movement (marching in time) feels natural. Are you moving with the child, or just watching? Your physical participation matters — the child learns timing partly by mirroring you.

### 4. Dynamic Control
**Developing well:** The child can play a drum at three distinct volume levels (loud, medium, soft) on command. They can gradually get louder or softer without sudden jumps.

**Developing slowly:** The child plays at one volume regardless of instructions, or can only produce loud and soft with nothing in between.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you explicitly practiced the "hand height" game or similar dynamic exercises? Dynamic control is a motor skill and needs specific practice — it does not develop just from general percussion play.
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Treating Rhythm as Background

Rhythm is not the warm-up for "real" music lessons. It is the foundation. A child who moves on to singing or instrument study without a reliable internal pulse will struggle with everything — tuning, ensemble playing, reading notation — because they do not have the temporal framework that holds music together. Take this packet seriously and do not rush through it.

### Only Using Western Music

If the only music the child hears is Western pop, rock, and classical, they will develop a narrow rhythmic vocabulary centered on 4/4 time. The world's rhythmic traditions are far richer than this. West African drumming, Indian tabla, Brazilian samba, and Middle Eastern percussion all offer rhythmic patterns and organizational principles that expand the child's sense of what rhythm can be. You do not need expertise in these traditions — you need recordings and the willingness to play them.

### Correcting Instead of Modeling

When the child's beat drifts, do not say "you're speeding up." Instead, clap alongside them at the correct tempo and let them adjust to you. When their pattern echo is wrong, clap the correct pattern again rather than explaining what they got wrong. At this age, the ear learns from repeated accurate models, not from verbal corrections.

### Skipping Movement

Some mentors are uncomfortable moving to music themselves, so they skip the movement activities. Do not do this. Rhythm lives in the body, and a child who learns rhythm only through hand-clapping is learning a fraction of what is available. March, sway, step, and dance with the child. Your willingness to move is more important than your skill at moving.
mentor_guide.narration_evaluation:
### Prompt 1: What is a Beat?
**Strong:** Demonstrates a steady beat while explaining that a beat is the steady pulse underneath music. May compare it to a heartbeat or clock. Shows physical understanding, not just verbal.
**Weak:** Cannot demonstrate a steady beat while talking about it. Defines it vaguely: "it's the sound the music makes."

### Prompt 2: Pattern Creation
**Strong:** Claps a clear, repeatable pattern and then changes one specific element (makes a beat longer, adds a rest, changes a loud beat to soft). Can articulate what they changed.
**Weak:** Claps randomly. Cannot repeat their own pattern. Cannot identify what they changed.

### Prompt 3: Tempo
**Strong:** Demonstrates fast and slow clapping with clear tempo control at each speed. Describes the feeling difference: "fast feels exciting" or "slow feels like walking in snow."
**Weak:** Cannot maintain a steady beat at different tempos. Speeding up and slowing down are uncontrolled.

### Prompt 4: Dynamics
**Strong:** Shows clear physical difference in how they strike the drum for loud versus soft. Can describe what changes: "I hit it harder" or "I use less of my hand."
**Weak:** Cannot produce a clearly different volume on command. Does not connect the physical action to the sound result.
